coredata.dc:title: Unveiling the explosion potential of lithium-ion batteries: A quantitative approach to safety assessment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhenpo
Shan, Tongxin
Zhao, Shanyu
Malfait, Wim J.
coredata.dc:description: The safety of lithium-ion batteries is a critical and challenging focus of current research. This perspective article systematically summarized and compared evaluation methods for the trinitrotoluene-equivalent (TNT-equivalent) of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) based on various characteristic parameters and proposed a mechanism-driven calculation approach. Using experimental data for validation, the study input TNT-equivalent values derived from different methods into an explosion dynamics model to predict explosion pressures, identifying the optimal calculation method through comparison with measured data. Results showed that the mechanism-driven approach accurately predicts explosion characteristics at different SOCs, with errors below 3%. This method eliminates the need for complex dynamic testing while providing precise predictions by linking the explosion equivalent to intrinsic thermal runaway (TR) mechanisms. The findings contribute to building safety analysis databases, establishing testing standards, and supporting the safety design of battery systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Safety
Thermal runaway
Explosion equivalent
Mechanism-driven

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TNT-equivalent of LIBs obtained based on three different methods. (b) Geometric schematic diagram of LIBs explosion dynamics characterization model. (c) The grid of the simplified one-quarter model.

(a)–(c) Simulated explosion pressure profiles of monitoring point 1 vs. iterations number in numerical model. (a) Energy density-based method, (b) heat generation-based method, (c) explosion dynamics-based method). (d) Explosion pressure of the gas mixture under different SOC conditions in 22 ​L explosion-proof sphere. (e) Comparison of the experimental and simulated results.
Gas composition produced by TR of Li-ion cells with different states of charge (SOCs).

head.ce:title: Unveiling the explosion potential of lithium-ion batteries: A quantitative approach to safety assessment

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Building Energy Materials and Components
Swiss Federal Laboratories for Materials Science and Technology (Empa)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory for Building Energy Materials and Components, Swiss Federal Laboratories for Materials Science and Technology (Empa), Dubendorf, 8600, Switzerland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The safety of lithium-ion batteries is a critical and challenging focus of current research. This perspective article systematically summarized and compared evaluation methods for the trinitrotoluene-equivalent (TNT-equivalent) of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) based on various characteristic parameters and proposed a mechanism-driven calculation approach. Using experimental data for validation, the study input TNT-equivalent values derived from different methods into an explosion dynamics model to predict explosion pressures, identifying the optimal calculation method through comparison with measured data. Results showed that the mechanism-driven approach accurately predicts explosion characteristics at different SOCs, with errors below 3%. This method eliminates the need for complex dynamic testing while providing precise predictions by linking the explosion equivalent to intrinsic thermal runaway (TR) mechanisms. The findings contribute to building safety analysis databases, establishing testing standards, and supporting the safety design of battery systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the safety issues of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted widespread attention worldwide as the battery-powered devices are becoming ubiquitous. Especially in the fields of transportation and energy storage, LIBs are the most promising energy supply system and have gained significant dominance in the market due to their numerous advantages [1]. However, the high energy density of LIBs also poses serious risks, as they can release large amounts of energy under abusive conditions. The occurrence of a cascade of chemical reactions inside LIBs, leading to the release of significant heat and gas, is commonly referred to as thermal runaway (TR) [2]. Gas venting, fire, and explosion are all manifestations of TR, with explosion representing the most extreme scenario [3]. Currently, extensive research has been conducted on the TR of LIBs. However, capturing the extreme TR scenarios proves to be challenging due to the significant hazards and transient nature, necessitating further research in this field [4]. In addition, there is currently no unified standard or consensus regarding assessment methods and solutions for extreme TR hazards like fires and explosions [3,5].

Although some regulations and standards have been established to describe the hazards associated with battery TR. For instance, the Chinese standard GB 38031–2023 stipulates that the battery system must not catch fire or explode within 5 ​min after TR; a similar requirement is outlined by the United Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE) [6]. Meanwhile, the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC) provides only a broad definition of venting, describing it as the “release of excessive internal pressure from a cell/battery in a manner intended by design to preclude rupture or explosion” [7]. These approaches primarily focus on qualitative descriptions of observable phenomena rather than providing quantitative assessments of explosion hazards. A precise assessment method to quantify the risks of battery explosions from multiple perspectives remains lacking.

The existing studies have revealed that the root of the Li-ion batteries explosion lies in the generation of flammable and explosive gases during TR reactions [8,9], while the chain reactions mechanisms in this process are very complicated. In addition, Li-ion batteries comprises a variety of materials, and there currently exist no tools capable of directly modeling battery explosions. Consequently, it is essential to rely on key parameters that can accurately characterize this process to gain an intuitive understanding of the hazards, such as explosion equivalent. Evaluating the explosion equivalent of LIBs is crucial for quantitatively describing the risks associated with TR. This can provide intuitive insights and improve the comprehension of the severity of hazards, thus establishing a correlation between TR accidents and the intrinsic safety of the battery. Additionally, explosion equivalent can serve as an important parameter for establishing the characterization model of battery explosions. On this basis, the extreme risks associated with TR can be predicted and the corresponding measures can be presented, which contributes to the development of safer and more reliable battery systems.

Generally, trinitrotoluene-equivalent (TNT-equivalent) is a typical parameter to quantify the explosion process of energetic substances from the perspective of energy release. It serves as a convention for expressing energy and typically used to describe the energy released in an explosion. This convention aims to compare the destructiveness of an event with that of conventional explosive materials, of which TNT is a typical example. Therefore, it is also applicable to describe the hazards of Li-ion batteries explosion. By using TNT-equivalent, it facilitates the comparison of explosion potential among various batteries or energy storage systems. This comparative analysis assists in identifying and prioritizing safety considerations, guiding design improvements, and facilitating decision-making processes. Moreover, TNT is a common explosive compound with well-defined characteristics and properties. By comparing the explosion of a battery to the explosive power of TNT, we can standardize and quantify the intensity of the explosion, facilitating comprehension of the potential impact and laying the groundwork for establishing a characterization model.

Researchers have made efforts to quantify the explosion hazards of LIBs. However, the calculation methods of the TNT-equivalent of Li-ion batteries in previous studies are mainly based on the energy density and heat generation of the cells [10,11]. These approaches, while useful for basic estimations, are limited in scope and cannot offer a comprehensive explanation of Li-ion battery explosion. Notably, they fail to fully consider the energy release paths and characteristics specific to explosion events (e.g. the impact of shockwaves), which are critical to accurately assessing the associated risks. As a result, the calculated TNT-equivalents often exhibit significant errors. The detailed verification and analysis of the limitations of energy density- and heat generation-based methods will be provided in the following sections.

Thus, this study summarized and compared TNT-equivalent assessment methods based on different characterization parameters and proposed a mechanism-driven method for calculating explosion equivalent of LIBs. This method linked the TNT-equivalent of the battery to its intrinsic explosion mechanism. The verification results from the finite element characterization model showed that, in contrast to other methods, the mechanism-driven method can accurately predict and characterize the explosion characteristics of LIBs. The aim of this study is to provide theoretical tools for the safety design of battery systems.

There is no doubt that TNT and LIBs are different chemical systems, so for their explosion problems, the explosion sources are different. However, for the explosion of energetic materials, the prerequisites, energy release forms, and the hazards are the same. Therefore, the comparison was carried out based on the universal parameters that can typify explosion phenomena. Currently, there are mainly three methods to calculate the TNT-equivalent (W TNT) of Li-ion batteries.

Energy density is the amount of energy stored within a specified volume or mass. One approach to estimating the TNT-equivalent is by comparing the energy density of the LIBs (E
m-cell) to that of TNT (E
m-TNT). However, it is important to note that this method serves as an estimation rather than an accurate measure of the actual explosive potential.
(1)
W TNT
=
E
m − cell
⋅
m cell
E
m − TNT
where m cell is the mass of the battery.

Since the TR process produces substantial heat, the TNT-equivalent can be also determined based on the reaction heat (ΔH), which is regarded as the explosion heat when the battery explodes. According to the measured maximum temperature (T max) during explosion of LIBs and the initial temperature (T 0), ΔH and W TNT can be obtained:
(2)
Δ H =
C cell
m cell
(
T max
−
T 0
)
(3)
W TNT
=
Δ H
H TNT
where C cell is the specific heat capacity of the battery; H TNT is the explosion heat of TNT. Compared with the direct energy density-based method, this method further considers the hazards-causing mechanism of TR. However, while an improvement, this method is not flawless as, during the TR process, energy is not solely dissipated as heat but also generates high-pressure impacts on the surroundings. Hence, a comprehensive assessment methodology should incorporate the pathways of energy transfer to ensure thorough evaluation.

An explosion is the abrupt and violent release of energy, typically accompanied by the generation of shockwaves with high-temperature and high-pressure. Being the primary form of energy release during an explosion, critical parameters of the shockwaves, including temperature and pressure, can characterize the explosion. Therefore, we focused on the fundamental traits of explosion, and established the connection between the LIB and its TNT-equivalent according to the critical parameter, specifically the explosion overpressure [3]:
(4)
Δ P =
a 1
Z
+
a 2
Z 2
+
a 3
Z 3
(5)
Z =
r
W TNT
1 / 3
where ΔP denotes the overpressure peak of shockwave; a 1, a 2, a 3 are the coefficients in this formula and they vary based on the specific testing conditions; r is the distance from the measuring point to the explosion center.

The difficulty with this method is that the measurement of shockwave overpressure can be challenging due to the hazardous and instantaneous nature of explosions. Usually, the prevailing approach to determine the pressure of a battery explosion involves the utilization of high-precision, fast-response dynamic pressure sensors for measurement. In this regard, since previous studies have identified the generation of flammable gases during thermal runaway as the root cause of Li-ion cell explosions, establishing a link between the TNT-equivalent and the intrinsic characteristics of the battery and its explosion mechanism is essential. As described in our previous study, explosion pressure of the flammable gas mixture can be predicted by the chemical reaction kinetics model and explosion physics model [12,13], based on the specific composition of the released gas. Thus, combined with the above Eqs. (4) and (5), TNT-equivalent of LIBs can be obtained by taking the intrinsic explosion mechanism into consideration.

In the following, experimental data from Ref. [12] will be used for method validation and analysis, where the overheat-to- TR tests were conducted on 42 Ah commercial Li-ion cells. The TR was induced in a sealed metal canister under isothermal conditions (250 ​°C) to collect vented gas for subsequent explosion tests. Gas composition analysis was performed using gas chromatography (GC), and the explosion characteristics (e.g., laminar flame speed, detonation pressure) were measured in an explosion-proof sphere. The explosion pressure calculated from theoretical models will be compared with the experimental results.

In this section, the above three methods were employed to calculate the TNT-equivalent of LIBs tested in Ref. [12]. The tested samples were commercial prismatic Li-ion cells with a capacity of 42 Ah and a nominal voltage of 3.7 ​V. The voltage range is 2.8–4.2 ​V. The cathode material was Li [Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3]O2 (NCM 111), and the anode was graphite. The conditioning tests using constant current and constant voltage (CC-CV) mode were performed on the cells to ensure consistency. Initial states of charge (SOCs) were adjusted to 30%, 50%, and 100% by discharging with CC mode. These SOC levels were chosen to represent low, medium, and high energy states, providing a comprehensive analysis of TR and explosion characteristics.

Then the calculation results of different methods were used as inputs to the explosion dynamics characterization model (carried out in ANSYS-AUTODYN, a professional software for explosion simulation) that we built previously [13] to predict the shockwave pressure during battery explosion. The predicted results were compared with the explosion pressure measured in our previous research [12] to validate the accuracy of the calculation results of TNT-equivalent. It should be noted that, for the explosion dynamics-based method, the TNT-equivalent was not directly calculated from the measured pressure. Instead, the gas composition analysis results in Ref. [12] served as the explosion source in the mixed gases reaction kinetics model (coded through Python) to calculate the pressure, thereby achieving mechanism-driven battery explosion equivalent quantification. The detailed analysis and verification process are illustrated in Fig. 1 , and the gas composition analysis results can be found in Table 1 from Ref. [12]. It should be noted that the data shown in Table 1 were obtained from experiments conducted on 42 Ah Li-ion cells with NCM111 cathode at different SOCs: 30%, 50%, and 100%. TR tests were triggered by heating the accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC) chamber from an initial temperature of 25 ​°C–250 ​°C and maintaining a constant temperature of 250 ​°C in isothermal mode. This controlled temperature increase ensured the initiation of exothermic reactions within the cells, thereby triggering the TR process. For detailed experimental settings, see Ref. [12].

Fig. 2 (a) displays the results of TNT-equivalents calculated through different methods. Obviously, the values of TNT-equivalent increased with the ascend of SOC values when using different calculation methods. This was because more energy was stored inside the Li-ion cells at higher SOC values, which was also released during TR. Besides, the specific values of TNT-equivalent calculated by different methods varied greatly although their changing trends were similar. Next, the calculated TNT-equivalents were set to be the explosion sources in the numerical model to interpret the explosion characteristics. Fig. 2(b) depicts the geometric model of the numerical simulation. Based on the actual setup and size of real test scenario, the spherical computational domain was built and the explosion source was set to be spherical to produce stable and uniform shockwaves, thereby simulating the explosion behavior of flammable gas mixture released by LIBs in the explosion-proof sphere. Three monitoring points were positioned according to the location of pressure sensor on the explosion-proof sphere, which can record the explosion pressure throughout the simulation. Since the entire numerical model was symmetrical, it was set in a quarter plane to reduce the computational workload. The grid of the simplified quarter model is shown in Fig. 2(c) with a size of 1 ​mm. The initial and boundary conditions of this model were imposed based on the actual test scenarios. For details on other specific control equations and parameter configurations, refer to Ref. [13].

The simulated explosion pressure profiles calculated using the TNT-equivalents obtained by different methods as input are shown in Fig. 3 (a)–(c). It can be found that in any method, the peak value of explosion pressure increased with the rise of SOC, which was consistent with the change trend in Fig. 2(a). The same situation can also be seen in Fig. 3(d), which depicts the explosion pressure of the gas mixture generated by Li-ion cells at different SOC values in 22 ​L explosion-proof sphere. The simulated explosion pressure profiles exhibited a similar trend to the actual measured results.

Additionally, Fig. 3(e) compares the peak values of explosion overpressure between the simulation results and the experimentally measured results. It was obvious that the overpressure values calculated using the TNT-equivalents obtained from the energy density and the heat generation deviated greatly from the actual values. In contrast, the use of TNT-equivalent based on explosion dynamics can provide more accurate predictions of explosion overpressure. Obviously, the energy density-based and heat generation-based methods greatly overestimate the explosion equivalent of Li-ion cells. This was because during the explosion of the LIB, the energy stored inside was released not only as high temperature, but also in the form of high-pressure shockwaves, causing mechanical impact to the surroundings. This discrepancy arises because the energy density-based method relies solely on the stored energy within the cells for a simple estimation, while the heat generation-based method only considers the high temperature effect. Neither of them considers the specific energy release paths and forms during explosion. However, in real explosion scenarios, the stored energy is partitioned into heat generation, and mechanical impact caused by high-pressure shockwaves, which both account for the majority of explosion hazards. Therefore, it was inaccurate to evaluate the explosion equivalent of LIBs only from the perspective of energy density or heat production. In contrast, the TNT-equivalent calculation method based on explosion dynamics fully considered the characteristics of the explosion source and the energy transfer paths during the explosion process of Li-ion cells.

Further, Fig. 3(e) shows the relative errors between the overpressure simulated using the explosion dynamics method and the actual values. It can be found that the errors were always less than 3%, indicating that this method can accurately predict the explosion characteristics of LIBs and was considered to be the best method to evaluate the TNT-equivalent of LIBs.

This study presented a mechanism-driven method for calculating the TNT-equivalent of LIBs, based on experimental data and explosion dynamics. The proposed method can accurately predict explosion characteristics of LIBs, with deviations below 3% at different SOCs. This method simplifies the calculation process, avoiding complex dynamic testing while maintaining high accuracy.

It is important to note that this study is primarily a perspective article aiming to propose a novel mechanism-driven method for calculating the explosion equivalent of LIBs, which focuses on presenting a theoretical framework. Future research will aim to expand the applicability of the proposed method to real-life battery scenarios by considering diverse battery chemistries and packagings, varying environmental factors (e.g., temperature, pressure, humidity), and external damage conditions. Moreover, standardized dimensionless parameters should be introduced to enhance the generalizability. These efforts will further validate the proposed approach and provide comprehensive tools for battery safety assessment. Additionally, a more detailed, quantitative analysis of the individual impacts of each gas component on explosion pressure will be a key focus of the future research, which will significantly enhance the accuracy of safety assessments and the understanding of explosion dynamics in battery systems.

Key findings include:
(i)
In terms of battery safety assessment:

This method takes the mixed gas components produced by LIBs under various abuse conditions as input, solves the explosion overpressure at this time through the chemical reaction kinetics model programmed in Python, and then calculates the TNT-equivalents based on explosion physics theories, simplifying safety evaluations and ensuring reliability.

Further, the findings offer a foundation for establishing safety standards for LIBs. Reasonable standards should cover key indicators such as the TNT-equivalent, explosion overpressure and shockwave profile, and gas composition during TR. Standards should also incorporate threshold values for these parameters, tailored to different operational states and abuse scenarios, ensuring that the risks of battery explosions can be systematically assessed and mitigated. Testing protocols based on the mechanism-driven method provide reliable and reproducible evaluations, contributing to the formulation of robust regulatory frameworks for LIBs safety.
(ii)
In terms of battery intrinsic safety:

This approach establishes a link between the reactions of battery component materials and the TR hazards, which provides an effective approach to develop a safety analysis database for LIBs, offering an intuitive understanding of the LIBs safety. This is of great significance for optimizing battery design and improving the intrinsic safety of power batteries.
(iii)
In terms of battery safety applications:

Integrating the explosion equivalent of LIBs with an explosion characterization model allows for accurately predicting the physical field variations (e.g., temperature, pressure) during TR, enabling a clearer understanding of the interaction between the battery and the thermal insulation management system and a system-level optimization.

In summary, this study provides theoretical tools for enhancing the safety design and implementation of battery systems, and provides scientific reference and guidance for the formulation and development of relevant standards.

Zhenpo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Tongxin Shan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Formal analysis. Shanyu Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Wim J. Malfait: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52072040, U21A20170) and National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2022YFB3305400). T. Shan acknowledges the support from China Scholarship Council (No. 202306030127).

Adv Energy Mater
